data.title: Harbor Dividend Growth Leaders ETF Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
With 2026 underway, the market appears poised for a shift toward broader participation and more fundamental drivers of return. - Westfield Capital Management Company, L.P.

Market in Review

Equity markets posted modest gains in the fourth quarter of 2025, capping a year marked by early trade shocks, robust earnings, and a growing divergence between headline indexes and underlying market breadth. While large-cap technology names remained a focal point, the final months of 2025 saw renewed strength in small caps and cyclical sectors, driven by resilient consumer spending, a steepening yield curve, and continued disinflation. The Federal Reserve's ("Fed") dovish pivot and easing financial conditions further supported sentiment — even as investors grew more discerning about Artificial Intelligence ("AI")-driven growth — and labor market dynamics remained in flux.

Portfolio Performance

During the fourth quarter, the Harbor Dividend Growth Leaders ETF ("ETF") returned 3.47% ('NAV'), outperforming the style benchmark, the NASDAQ US Dividend Achievers Select Total Return Index ("Index"), which returned 1.53%, and its primary benchmark, the S&P 500 Index, which returned 2.66%.

From a sector perspective, relative strength in Consumer Discretionary and Information Technology offset relative weakness within Communication Services and Financials.

The relative outperformance of the ETF, compared to the Index, was primarily driven by positive stock selection, but the ETF also benefited from a common factor tailwind during the quarter. From a factor perspective, the ETF's overweight exposures to earnings variation — a proxy for more cyclically oriented stocks — was a tailwind to performance, offsetting a headwind from being underweight growth relative to the Index.

Dividend growth strategies recovered ground in the fourth quarter, capping a year marked by stark contrasts between speculative rallies and quality-led resilience. Throughout much of 2025, markets were dominated by narrow leadership and risk-seeking behavior, particularly in the first half of the year, when momentum-driven trades and high-beta stocks outpaced fundamentals. Dividend growth — by design, a quality and defensively oriented strategy — lagged during these periods as investors' focus skewed toward thematic exposure and near-term upside.

However, the backdrop began to shift meaningfully in the second half of the year. The Fed's pivot toward rate cuts, alongside easing inflation, a stable consumer, and moderating labor trends, helped improve financial conditions without reigniting excess. In the fourth quarter, market breadth broadened materially: Small- and mid-cap stocks outperformed mega caps, cyclicals gained traction, and sector leadership rotated toward Financials, Industrials, and Health Care — areas more aligned with dividend growth portfolios.

By year-end, the market appeared to be transitioning from a liquidity-fueled, speculative phase toward one increasingly driven by earnings quality and valuation discipline. While full-year returns favored early-cycle risk taking, the late-year pivot reinforced the long-term appeal of companies with durable cash flows, strong balance sheets, and a demonstrated commitment to dividend growth. As investor positioning normalizes and volatility remains episodic, we believe the foundation is in place for quality dividend strategies to outperform in the next phase of the cycle.

Contributors & Detractors

The top five contributors to relative performance during the quarter were: Mueller Industries, Cardinal Health (CAH), Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group (SMFG), Expedia, and Tapestry. (TPR)

Mueller Industries, a manufacturer of materials and components used in industrial applications, outperformed during the quarter, as the company reported strong quarterly results with net income and earnings-per-share ("EPS") growth ahead of Wall St. expectations. We continue to believe that the company's durable end-market positioning in plumbing, HVAC/R, and industrial components should support growth through mixed cycles. We did not make any adjustments to our position during the quarter.

Cardinal Health, a distributor of medical and lab supplies, outperformed on the back of a strong earnings report, driven by earnings growth ahead of investor expectations and raised fiscal year 2026 guidance. We maintain conviction in the thesis but reduced our position on strength during the quarter.

Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group, a dominant Japanese bank, outperformed during the quarter as the company reported strong second-quarter earnings results, with EPS significantly ahead of Wall St. expectations. We did not make any adjustments to our position during the quarter.

Expedia, an online travel company, outperformed during the quarter following strong third-quarter earnings, with EPS and sales ahead of expectations. We did not make any adjustments to our position during the quarter.

Tapestry, a leading luxury accessories and lifestyle company, was a key contributor within the Consumer Discretionary sector. Shares advanced on continued strength from the Coach brand, whose ongoing brand elevation and successful innovation strategy continued to resonate with consumers, supporting strong top-line growth and margin leverage. Increased desirability enabled meaningful price increases without promotional pressure, while the brand's ability to attract and retain younger customers reinforced potential long-term relevance. We did not make any adjustments to our position during the quarter.

The top five detractors from relative performance during the quarter were: Arthur J. Gallagher & Co, Meta Platforms, International Paper, Williams Companies, and Cintas (CTAS).

Arthur J. Gallagher & Co, an insurance brokerage and risk management service provider, underperformed following a disappointing earnings report stating that the company missed its earnings target, driven by slower-than-anticipated organic growth. While we continue to like the long-term story, we decided to step aside from the name for the time being, as a source of funds for other names that we believe have a better risk/reward profile.

Meta Platforms, a social media platform, underperformed during the quarter as investor concern around lack of visibility into 2026 expense growth and return on investment from AI CapEx spending weighed on sentiment. While we continue to believe that the company is well positioned to emerge as a leader in AI with a long runway for revenue growth, we decided to step aside from the name for the time being, as a source of funds for other names that we believe have a better risk/reward profile.

International Paper, a global producer of fiber-based packaging and pulp, underperformed over the period following a disappointing earnings report and lowered guidance for 2026 and 2027, driven by macro headwinds from consumer and industrial weakness. Despite operational improvements and progress in the company's turnaround story, we decided to step aside as the turnaround unfolds and monitor for clearer evidence that operational improvements are translating into sustainable performance.

Williams Companies, an energy infrastructure company specializing in natural gas and petroleum, underperformed after reporting EPS slightly below consensus expectations. Despite the recent underperformance, we maintain conviction in the potential role of midstream natural gas in a world involving power infrastructure build-out driven by AI demands. We added to our position during the quarter.

Cintas, a provider of corporate uniforms, apparel, and supplies, underperformed alongside broader defensive services, despite reporting positive earnings results during the quarter. In our view, fundamentals remain intact with an attractive business model, with steady free-cash-flow generation and a path to margin expansion. We did not make any adjustments to our position during the quarter.

Buy and Sells

Looking forward, consensus points to more balanced EPS growth across market segments in 2026, supporting a healthier and less concentrated earnings backdrop. Cyclicals are trading at a meaningful discount and showing accelerating profit growth, creating attractive opportunities where fundamentals are improving faster than prices.

Along these lines, we have become more constructive on the Energy sector, given oil prices, valuations, and sentiment, and added exposure to the sector during the quarter. For example, we purchased Diamondback Energy (FANG), an independent oil and gas company concentrating on the acquisition, development, and production of unconventional onshore oil and natural gas reserves in the Permian Basin.

Within Financials, we added exposure to banks with the purchase of Citigroup (C) and sold our position in insurance brokerage and risk management service provider, Arthur J. Gallagher, as a source of funds.

We also added biopharma exposure within Health Care, where we added a number of new positions during the quarter, including global pharmaceutical companies Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) and AbbVie (ABBV). Large pharmaceutical companies have faced a multiyear overhang from most-favored-nations pricing that has resulted in the opportunity to buy good growth stories at attractive relative valuations, in our opinion.

In contrast, we reduced exposure to Real Estate during the quarter and sold our positions in Welltower (WELL), a health care REIT, and STAG Industrial (STAG), an industrial REIT, as sources of funds for other names that we believe have better risk/reward profiles.

Sector Overweights and Underweights

Communication Services represented the ETF's largest overweight relative to the Index entering 2025. We subsequently sold both positions that we held entering 2025 and do not have any exposure to Communications Services as of year-end 2025. This is in line with the Index, which also does not have any exposure to that sector as of year-end.

The Consumer Staples sector represented the ETF's largest underweight entering 2025 and continues to be a large underweight as of year-end. Our view on the relative attractiveness of the sector did not change during the quarter. We remain underweight the sector, as we believe there are more compelling investment opportunities with more attractive growth profiles in other sectors.

It is important to remember that we take a bottom-up, fundamental approach when identifying securities for inclusion within the ETF, and sector exposures are the result of our fundamental conviction in individual companies. Additionally, we continue to believe that having a healthy balance of high-quality cyclical- and secular-growth companies provides more consistent results over time.

Outlook

With 2026 underway, the market appears poised for a shift toward broader participation and more fundamental drivers of return. Monetary policy is likely to remain supportive, and earnings expectations suggest a more balanced contribution across sectors and size segments. However, persistent macro uncertainties including labor softness, elevated valuations, and episodic volatility underscore the importance of maintaining a selective and risk-aware approach. In our view, a focus on high-quality businesses with improving fundamentals and valuation support will remain essential in this evolving environment.

Risks

Unlike mutual funds, ETFs may trade at a premium or discount to their net asset value.

Investments involve risk including the possible loss of principal. There is no guarantee the investment objective of the Fund will be achieved. The Fund's emphasis on dividend paying stocks involves the risk that such stocks may fall out of favor with investors and under-perform the market. There is no guarantee that a company will pay or continually increase its dividend. The Fund may invest in a limited number of companies or at times may be more heavily invested in particular sectors. As a result, the Fund's performance may be more volatile, and the value of its shares may be especially sensitive to factors that specifically effect those sectors. The Fund may invest in foreign securities which may be more volatile and less liquid due to currency fluctuation, political instability, government sanctions, social and economic risks. Foreign currencies can decline in value and can adversely affect the dollar value of the Fund.

Benchmarks

The S&P 500 Index is an unmanaged index generally representative of the U.S. market for large capitalization equities. The NASDAQ U.S. Dividend Achievers Select® Index is a modified market capitalization weighted index. The NASDAQ U.S. Dividend Achievers Select® Index is comprised of a select group of securities with at least ten consecutive years of increasing annual regular dividend payments. These unmanaged indices do not reflect fees and expenses and are not available for direct investment.

Disclosures

On or about May 20, 2022, the Fund acquired the assets and assumed the then existing known liabilities of the Predecessor Fund and the Fund is expected to be the performance successor of the reorganization. This means that the Predecessor Fund's performance and financial history will be used by the Fund going forward from the date of reorganization.

In the reorganization, former shareholders of the Predecessor Fund received shares of the Fund. Accordingly, the performance of the Fund for periods prior to the reorganization is the performance of the Predecessor Fund and the performance shown for periods prior to July 26, 2013 is the performance of a private investment vehicle that predated the Predecessor Fund. The private investment vehicle was managed by the Subadvisor using investment policies, objectives and guidelines that were in all material respects equivalent to the management of the Fund and Predecessor Fund. However, the private investment vehicle was not a registered investment company and so it was not subject to the same investment and tax restrictions as the Fund and Predecessor Fund. If it had been, its performance may have been lower. The performance of the Predecessor Fund has not been restated to reflect the annual operating expenses of the Fund, which are lower than those of the Predecessor Fund. Because the Fund has different fees and expenses than the Predecessor Fund, the Fund would also have had different performance results.

Expense ratio information is as of the Fund's current prospectus, as supplemented. Gross expenses are the Fund's total annual operating expense.

Due to the security valuation procedures of the Fund and intra-day trading activity not included in the FactSet calculations, the actual returns may vary. From time to time the cash return in the portfolio may appear distorted based on the way FactSet's attribution calculation methodology addresses delayed settlements.

Best and Worst Performers sections reflect stocks in the portfolio for the quarter with an average weight of 0.25% or greater.

This information should not be considered as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. The weightings, holdings, industries, sectors, countries, and returns mentioned may change at any time and may not represent current or future investments.

Views expressed herein are drawn from commentary provided to Harbor by the subadvisor and may not be reflective of their current opinions or future actions, are subject to change without prior notice, and should not be considered investment advice.

As a result of changing market conditions, expenses and other statistics may change at any time and may differ from those shown.

Active Currency Contribution is the Currency Contribution of the portfolio minus the Currency Contribution of the benchmark.

Allocation Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return that is attributable to taking different group bets from the benchmark. (If either the portfolio or the benchmark has no position in a given group, allocation effect is the lone effect.) A group's allocation effect equals the average percent capitalization of the portfolio's group minus the average percent cap of the benchmark's group times the total return of the benchmark group minus the total return of the benchmark.

Average Weight is the dollar value (price times the shares held) of the security or group, divided by the total dollar value of the entire portfolio displayed as a percentage. It is calculated as the simple arithmetic average of daily values.

Contribution to Return is the contribution of a security or group to the overall portfolio return. It is calculated as the security weight multiplied by the daily security return linked daily across the reporting period.

Currency Contribution is Total Return in USD subtracting out the Local Returns.

Local Returns are the Total Return of the portfolio or benchmark using the local currency.

Selection Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return attributable to choosing different securities within groups from the benchmark. A group's security selection effect equals the average weight of the benchmark's group times the total return of the portfolio's group minus the total return of the benchmark's group.

Total Effect is the sum of Allocation Effect and Selection Effect. The total effect represents the opportunity cost of what was done in a group relative to the overall portfolio. It is not just the difference between percent contribution in the portfolio and benchmark. At the overall portfolio level, the two numbers are equal. At the group level, they can be different.

Total Return is the price change of a security or group including dividends accrued over the report period (or the in-portfolio return) which includes only the time period that each security was in the portfolio.

Definitions

Free cash flow represents the cash a company can generate after accounting for capital expenditures needed to maintain or maximize its asset base.

Beta is a measure of systematic risk, or the sensitivity of a fund to movements in the benchmark. A beta of 1 implies that the expected movement of a fund's return would match that of the benchmark used to measure beta.

Earnings Per Share ('EPS') is the portion of a company's profit allocated to each outstanding share.

Capital expenditure (CapEx) refers to money spent on major, long-term business assets such as property, equipment, or technology.

data.title: Can Emerging Markets ETFs Enhance Your Technology Exposure?
data.description:
Emerging Markets technology is becoming a critical driver of global innovation, offering investors exposure to AI, semiconductors, and digital infrastructure while trading at lower price-to-earnings multiples than US technology.

US equity valuations remain elevated, and concentration risk is now at a decade-high, driven by a narrow group of mega-cap technology stocks. While we believe US innovation leadership remains strong, some forward-looking portfolios may consider diversification into areas where fundamentals have shown improvement and valuations have been lower relative to US technology, based on selected metrics, such as emerging markets technology.*

We believe the emerging markets (EM) technology sector represents one of the most compelling allocation opportunities right now. Here is why:

A Potentially Weaker US Dollar Supports Emerging Markets Technology Firms.

A softer US dollar has historically been supportive of emerging markets technology and broader EM assets, as local currency cash flows translate into more attractive returns for dollar‑based investors. When the dollar is weaker or more range‑bound, financial conditions in many EM economies tend to improve, lowering funding pressures and supporting equity market performance. Policy signals from the current US administration, including its focus on domestic manufacturing and reshoring, imply a tolerance—if not a preference—for a more competitive dollar to support exports, which indirectly enhances the backdrop for emerging markets technology companies linked to global trade and supply chains.

AI Capex Is Powering Asian Emerging Markets Supply Chains.

While artificial intelligence (AI) demand is often discussed through the lens of US mega‑caps, much of the physical build‑out of AI systems actually happens in Asia, led by emerging markets technology companies in semiconductors, memory, and advanced packaging. EM technology firms, including Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSMC) and SK Hynix, play important roles in global AI hardware and memory supply chains and are exposed to ongoing AI capital expenditure ("AI Capex") across data centers, semiconductors, and advanced computing. American companies contract out the manufacturing of their chips to these companies.

Emerging Markets Technology Could Be at an Early Stage of A Potential New Profit Growth Cycle.

Many EM technology companies recently executed inventory corrections and cost rationalization that have been viewed positively by analysts.1 Today, we are seeing improving profitability expectations, operating leverage, and balance-sheet metrics, alongside lower price-to-earnings multiples than those of US technology peers.

Analysts’ estimates for long-term earnings per share (EPS) growth have also picked up significantly since June, placing them on par with the average estimates for US technology companies.

Due to high concentration in US equities,2 stretched valuations, and AI investment broadening globally,3 we believe EM technology represents a differentiated way to participate in long-term growth themes as part of a broader diversification approach.*

How to invest in Emerging Markets technology

The KraneShares Emerging Markets Consumer Technology ETF (KEMQ) provides targeted exposure to technology and internet companies across Asia and other emerging markets, positioning investors for this next phase of the global tech cycle. The KraneShares SSE STAR Market 50 Index ETF (KSTR) is a potential option for investors to dial-up their exposure to China’s technology sector in addition to broader emerging markets. KSTR provides exposure to the 50 largest companies listed on China’s STAR Market, which is dedicated to companies innovating in information technology, biomedicine, and new energy.

Key holdings in KEMQ include:

SK Hynix (HXSC.F)(4.3% of Net Assets as of 1/28/2026)

SK Hynix is a South Korean semiconductor manufacturer that specializes in memory chips such as DRAM and NAND flash, as well as related products like SSDs and image sensors. DRAM (dynamic random‑access memory) is the high‑speed, short‑term working memory that computers and servers use to store data they are actively processing, which must be refreshed constantly to retain information. NAND flash is a non-volatile type of memory that keeps data even when power is turned off and is widely used in solid‑state drives and other storage devices. SK Hynix is one of the world’s largest memory chip makers (commonly ranked second after Samsung) and a key supplier to major global technology companies and data centers, and in recent years it has drawn particular attention for its advanced AI memory, including high‑bandwidth memory (HBM) used in cutting‑edge AI accelerators.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (TSMC) (TSM)(3.76% of Net Assets as of 1/28/2026)

TSMC is a Taiwanese semiconductor company that operates as the world’s leading dedicated contract chip manufacturer, or “pure‑play” foundry. It fabricates advanced logic chips for major global customers, including Apple, Nvidia, AMD, and Qualcomm, powering products from smartphones to cloud data centers and AI supercomputers. TSMC is notable both for its dominant share of the global foundry market and for its cutting-edge process technologies used in the most advanced AI and high-performance computing chips.

Alibaba (BABA)(3.48% of Net Assets as of 1/28/2026)

Alibaba is a China-based technology platform covering E-Commerce, AI cloud services, logistics, media, and local services. According to publicly reported data, Alibaba’s Qwen large language model (LLM) is one of the most used models in China and is highly sought after by global corporations.4

Key holdings in KSTR include:

Cambricon Technologies (10.6% of Net Assets as of 1/28/2026)

A China-based AI chip designer. Cambricon’s processors are increasingly used in local AI servers as China-based companies expand domestic AI computing infrastructure.

Hygon Information (9.9% of Net Assets as of 1/28/2026)

Hygon is a key x86 CPU provider serving China’s data center and AI server markets. Hygon helps power the core computing engines that run cloud and AI workloads. An x86 CPU is a general‑purpose processor based on the widely used x86 instruction set architecture, originally developed for personal computers and now standard in most servers. Because so much existing software, operating systems, and cloud infrastructure is built around x86, these CPUs are the workhorses of modern data centers. Hygon's processors are used in domestic cloud and AI infrastructure as enterprises and hyperscalers expand domestic compute capacity.

Montage Technology (MNTCF) (6.4% of Net Assets as of 1/28/2026)

A critical supplier of memory interface and connectivity chips used in AI servers, Montage plays a behind‑the‑scenes but essential role in enabling high‑performance AI workloads. DDR5, the latest generation of double data rate (DDR) memory, allows data to be transferred much faster and more efficiently between processors and memory than prior standards, which is vital for training and running large AI models. By providing DDR5‑compatible memory interface chips and data‑center connectivity solutions, Montage helps ensure that AI servers can move and process the massive volumes of data required for modern artificial intelligence.

*Diversification does not ensure a profit or guarantee against a loss.

Citations:

  1. Medeiros, Gustavo. “A new bull market cycle in Emerging Market equities,” Ashmore. September 2024. Retrieved on 12/31/2025.
  2. Based on the weight of the top ten constituents in the S&P 500 Index as a percentage of the total index. Data from Bloomberg as of 12/31/2025.
  3. “Economy.” The 2025 AI Index Report, Stanford HAI. December 31, 2025.
  4. Jiang, Ben. "Alibaba's Qwen family hits 700 million downloads to lead global open-source AI adoption," South China Morning Post. January 12, 2026.

Definitions:

Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio: A stock valuation metric that shows how much investors are willing to pay for each dollar of a company's earnings, calculated by dividing the current share price by the earnings per share. It helps assess if a stock is overvalued (high P/E) or undervalued (low P/E) compared to its peers or its own history, often indicating growth expectations (higher P/E) or perceived risk (lower P/E).

Nasdaq 100 Index: The Nasdaq-100 Index tracks 100 of the largest non-financial companies listed on the NASDAQ stock market, including major tech, retail, and healthcare firms, and was launched on January 31, 1985, as a benchmark for innovation-driven growth, not including financial firms.

Earnings Per Share (EPS): A company's total profit over a 12-month period divided by the number of shares outstanding.

Est. 3-5 Year EPS Growth: Annualized growth expected for a company's earnings per share over at least a three-year period. For the purposes of this communication, publicly available estimates as far into the future as five but no less than three years are used for the constituents of an index or holdings of a fund to generate a harmonized average.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Sandisk: The Bust Cycle Could Be Coming Sooner Than Expected
data.description:
Sandisk (SNDK) is well known to many consumers for its memory cards used in electronic devices. However, the company is increasingly focused on providing storage solutions for AI data centers. The boom in AI capex has greatly benefited this company, causing the share price to surge. I believe the easy money has been made in this stock, and shareholders could be disappointed with expectations now so high. After a huge surge in the share price, a lot of good news is already priced into this stock. Furthermore, even a hint of a slowdown in AI capex spend could cause a major correction in this stock. Considering this, let's examine why this stock has surged, creating fortunes for many shareholders, and why significant downside risks may emerge going forward.

The Chart

Take a look at the chart below, and you might think you are looking at Nvidia (NVDA), which some believe is the "poster child" for the AI trade. However, Nvidia shares have been stuck in a trading range for months. In contrast, as the chart below shows, Sandisk shares have surged. This stock traded for less than $50 per share as recently as August, but it now trades for about $625 per share. This stock is overbought and well above the 50-day moving average, which is around $382. The current share price is nearly 4 times the 200-day moving average, which is around $164.

Why Fortunes Have Been Made

The hyperscalers have launched an unprecedented "arms race" to develop AI infrastructure, creating a boom for memory and storage companies like Sandisk. The memory and storage industry was unprepared for the AI capex spending surge, and this has led to huge demand and expanding profit margins as companies like Sandisk take advantage of the market. Sandisk has reported big Q2FY26 revenue increases across multiple segments of its business, which include: Consumer with a 39% quarter-over-quarter increase, or "QoQ", Edge with a 21% QoQ increase, and a whopping 64% QoQ increase for data center.

In 2024, Sandisk reported adjusted earnings of $2.14 per share. In 2025, the company's adjusted earnings surged to $22.40 per share. As shown below, the company is guiding for $12 to $14 in earnings for Q3. All of this shows how this stock surge has been driven by a surge in earnings. However, there is a real risk that this trend is not sustainable and that earnings will begin to decline by 2028, if not sooner. I believe that the risk for investors is that this stock will likely decline well before earnings begin to decline, and that is why I would cash in on the unprecedented gains this stock has seen.

Earnings Estimates And The Balance Sheet

As provided by Seeking Alpha, the earnings estimates below show that earnings are expected to surge from just under $40 per share in 2026 to over $80 per share in 2027. However, earnings are expected to start declining again in 2028, dropping nearly 10%. Stocks that have earnings declines typically trade at very low PE multiples. While the PE multiple is currently near 16, it is expected to drop to just below 9 times the 2028 estimates. The problem is that earnings could continue to decline significantly beyond 2028, as the storage industry potentially faces a combination of reduced demand and increased market supply, which could punish profit margins. Historically, these boom-and-bust cycles have occurred in the past. During each boom cycle, some have said this time is different, but I am skeptical of this view.

As for the balance sheet, Sandisk has about $1.54 billion in cash and around $813 million in debt. This balance sheet strength will help Sandisk weather the next potential cyclical downturn.

Why Fortunes Could Be Lost

If you look at the history of the memory and storage industry, you will see that boom cycles have never been sustainable for long; this is why the industry is known for boom and bust cycles. This boom cycle could have further to run, but I believe the easy money has been made and potential downside risks are mounting. Investors who choose to buy at current levels and shareholders who decide to continue holding this stock could be putting themselves at the mercy of the cycle. At current levels, I believe buying or holding this stock could be a nail-biter.

Many of the hyperscalers announced major increases to their capex budgets in early 2026. This has resulted in additional gains for Sandisk and suggests the AI capex boom will likely continue for the foreseeable future. However, several major potential roadblocks could impede these very optimistic spending plans. Let's take a closer look below:

1) Power Supply: While there has been a shortage of chips and storage lately, it seems to be shifting toward a looming electricity shortage. If companies cannot secure the power demands for their AI data center builds, these plans may need to be scaled back or put on hold entirely. Data centers now draw power equivalent to entire nuclear reactors (typically 1 GW or more per site), which is straining the power grid.

There is also a growing consumer backlash against AI data centers due to the impact on utility rates, and this has caused politicians to propose legislation that could impede data center growth. Estimates suggest a massive gap between what AI data centers need and what the current U.S. power grid can provide. If power isn't going to be available, the recently announced capex spending plans could need significant cuts. One major power company summarizes the challenges ahead by stating:

"Data centers now consume 4.4% of U.S. electricity, more than some entire states – and will reach double or even triple that amount by 2028. Meanwhile, commercial (2.6%) and industrial (2.1%) operations are growing at three times the rate of residential users. The grid, once a backbone, is now straining under the weight of power-hungry technologies and aging infrastructure. By 2028, the nation’s peak electricity supply will fall short of anticipated peak demand and by 2033, that gap will reach an estimated 175 GW capacity shortfall – the equivalent of the power used by 130 million homes!"

2) Efficiency Gains: There is a major potential shift whereby less demand for storage could be looming since building AI is more demanding than just running AI. There are also potential efficiency gains since researchers are working on breakthroughs in small model architectures that offer similar performance to the giant clusters requiring more storage. The small model architectures could require 90% less compute. When we see countries like China producing AI models like DeepSeek at a fraction of the cost compared to what the U.S. counterparts are producing, it seems likely that hyperscalers will eventually stop throwing money at AI and instead pivot to efficiency. This could lead to a period where companies stop buying new hardware and instead focus on optimizing existing assets.

3) Investors Appear Fed Up With AI Capex Spending: Companies like Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG), (GOOGL), and Meta Platforms (META) have all announced major recent increases in their capex spending plans in early 2026, and all of these stocks have plunged in value. Investors appear increasingly concerned about an AI bubble, as well as the fact that these capex spending plans are changing the balance sheets and free cash flows for these companies.

One of the objectives for a CEO is to create shareholder value, and the share price reaction has been so negative to these capex spending plans that it seems there could be a rethink of these plans in the coming quarters. This could result in CEOs cutting back on AI capex spending. In one example, Amazon lost nearly $500 billion in market cap in the 9 days after it announced a $200 billion capex spend for 2026. This is a hugely adverse reaction, and investors seem to be sending a clear message to CEOs to slow down or cut back.

4) CFOs Might Push Back: A recent Forrester report suggests that CFOs might not support the current pace of AI spending. The report suggests that only about 15% of AI decision-makers are seeing an increase in earnings over the past 12 months. The report goes on to suggest that AI spending might not be sustainable, and it states:

"Given the high payback expectations from AI investments now, we believe that CEOs will pull more CFOs into AI decisions, leading enterprises to delay 25% of their planned spend into 2027. The disconnect between the inflated promises of AI vendors and the value created for enterprises will force market correction."

Hyperscaler Demand Is The Primary Driver

I see the current rally in Sandisk as being driven by two factors. One is a general NAND price recovery, which provides a floor for margins, but hyperscaler demand seems to be the biggest factor in driving margins higher. Analysis suggests that approximately 60-65% of Sandisk's recent margin expansion is directly attributable to hyperscaler demand. Hyperscalers are now paying a significant premium for high-density SSDs to support AI training and inference. The company has reportedly doubled the price of these storage devices due to the severe supply shortage. With data center revenue rising 76% year on year, this has led to operating margins of 37.5%. The margins for NAND are significantly lower compared to the high-density SSDs that the hyperscalers are buying at elevated prices.

All of this suggests that if hyperscalers pull back on capex spending, revenues and margins could be very negatively impacted.

Current And Historical Valuation Levels

While Sandisk might look undervalued based solely on its price-to-earnings ratio, other valuation metrics suggest this stock could be grossly overvalued. Recent data suggests that Sandisk has a current price-to-book ratio of about 8.68 and the price-to-sales ratio is around 10.24. In prior cycle peaks, such as between 2017 and 2018, the price-to-sales ratio was around 3.5x and the price-to-book ratio was about 4.5x. Historical mid-cycle values are even lower than peaks, with price-to-sales ratios around 1.5x and price-to-book ratios being close to 2x. As shown in the charts below, Sandisk is now trading at extremely high valuation levels when considering these ratios. I would also point out that Western Digital (WDC) recently announced a $3.2 billion sale of Sandisk shares. Western Digital was once the parent company of Sandisk and this company has extensive experience dealing with boom and bust cycles in the storage industry. If Western Digital wants to sell this much Sandisk stock, it could be a warning sign for other investors to consider.

Where I Could Be Wrong

Although I believe that CEOs could soon reverse course on recently announced AI capex spending plans, there is a chance they could continue to increase it in the coming quarters. This could generate what I view as an even bigger fear of missing out, or "FOMO" rally in this stock. Amazon's CEO has suggested that the company will see returns on its AI investments, and if so, this could keep the AI party going. If AI capex spending continues longer than expected, this stock could reach the $1,000 price target set by some analysts.

In Summary

There's been an unprecedented boom in AI capex spending, which has led to an unprecedented surge in Sandisk shares. A combination of factors, including potential sharp cutbacks in capex spending, could send this stock significantly lower. Huge capex spending plans that were recently announced might not materialize due to challenges stemming from a lack of power, insufficient shareholder support, and a potential pivot toward efficiency, which could end or greatly slow down these plans.

I believe the hint of an AI capex spending slowdown could send this stock into a tailspin, especially as it trades at levels never seen before. I think it is important to remember that the stock market typically looks forward by 6 to 12 months. As such, it might not be long before investors start to anticipate the end of the storage demand boom. Considering this stock traded for less than $50 per share in August, there is a long way down. While I don't see it going that low any time soon, I also would not want to be the last person to leave this party.

data.title: Intuit: An Economic Moat Deepened By Artificial Intelligence
data.description:
SaaS-Pocalypse: An Overview

After researching Trade Desk (TTD), ServiceNow (NOW), and Adobe (ADBE) over the last few days, I continue my exploration of the software industry with Intuit (INTU), another victim of the 'SaaS-pocalypse'. Just to give you an idea of how negative market sentiment has become, approximately $1.0 trillion of market capitalization was erased from software stocks in the first seven trading days of February 2026.

From its all-time high of $117.99 reached in September 2025, the iShares Expanded Tech-Software ETF (IGV) is now trading below $83.00 per share, a decline of roughly 30% in just a few months. Intuit's share price has fared even worse, with a performance of -44% over the last six months, which is only slightly better than Trade Desk, the worst performer within my software coverage universe. However, after spending some time studying Intuit, I was surprised to discover how well-positioned the company is regarding artificial intelligence.

Similar to what I wrote on ServiceNow a few days ago, I would even say that artificial intelligence represents a massive opportunity for Intuit. Among software stocks, Intuit has been one of the worst performers, and this is despite having one of the best strategic positions to not only withstand disruption but to actually capitalize on it. In my view, this is where the opportunity is, where the disconnect is. Yes indeed, Intuit generates the vast majority of its revenues from a software-as-a-service business model, but that does not mean that its product lines are about to get disrupted anytime soon.

Not only do I view the current AI-related fears as overblown, but I also believe that mass adoption of AI agents is a tremendous opportunity for the ServiceNow AI Platform.

With the rise of artificial intelligence tools like OpenAI's ChatGPT or Anthropic's Claude, it is a fact that it has never been cheaper and easier to write code and build software. In this regard, I would agree that the barriers to entry of the software industry have been lowered by artificial intelligence. However, the ability to enter an industry is far from being equal to success.

Writing code may have been commodified, but what if the most successful software companies are not about selling code, but about selling a platform underpinned by a strong brand and reputation? Even if it is technically possible to build a nice little tax software similar to TurboTax out of a bedroom using artificial intelligence, does it mean that tens of millions of people will have enough trust and confidence to pour all their personal and financial information into it? I doubt it very much, and this is only one of the many reasons why I believe that AI-related fears around Intuit are way overstated at the moment.

Intuit's Lead In AI Deployment

Sasan Goodarzi became Intuit's chief executive officer effective January 1st, 2019. Just a few months later, in October 2019, the company announced a strategic and transformational shift to become an AI-driven platform. That was more than six years ago, well before the release of ChatGPT in November 2022. With such a forward-looking vision, I doubt that Intuit was caught by surprise by the ongoing shift toward AI agents. Even though we could debate the exact numbers included in the chart below, I think we can all agree on the structural trends that are highlighted, and this is good news for Intuit.

First of all, Intuit has developed its own generative AI operating system called GenOS that includes large language models specialized in finance-related tasks like taxes, accounting, or marketing, among others. Following my previous research on AI-related names like Microsoft (MSFT), Meta Platforms (META), or Nvidia (NVDA), I now understand that one of the most important limiting factors in the training of a new AI model is the amount of data available. Having a sufficient amount of computing power is also key, but what is the point of having the most advanced Nvidia GPUs if you do not have enough high-quality data to feed them?

In my view, this is where Intuit's significant competitive advantages become clear. Just imagine all the high-quality data that has been poured into TurboTax and QuickBooks over the last few decades. Who can compete against that? And let's also remember that without the data, you do not have the AI models either.

Now, when those AI models are put into practical use to perform real-world tasks, they become AI agents, and it is exactly what Intuit has been developing for years. In other words, Intuit is playing a key role in enabling AI models to perform real-world duties, and the financial world is the perfect field to do so.

In short, the fears of AI-related disruption surrounding Intuit are grossly exaggerated, at least in my view. Intuit is where the rubber meets the road, where AI models have been built on decades' worth of proprietary data to perform tasks in the real world. Yes indeed, it has never been easier and cheaper to write code, but Intuit is not in the business of writing and selling code in the first place. Also, what is the point of writing code if you do not have the data set to train them on?

An Impressive Financial Performance

Another element indicating that Intuit is not in the process of being disrupted is its incredible financial performance in fiscal 2025. Some might say that the impact of artificial intelligence has not been felt yet, but as discussed in the previous paragraph, what if the impact is actually a net positive? Nonetheless, Intuit delivered a 16% top-line growth in fiscal 2025, which was well above the 12% to 13% guidance range provided earlier in the year. This strangely reminds me of ServiceNow, another company that is supposed to be in the process of being disrupted but that also surpassed its 2025 guidance.

For the fourth quarter of 2025, ServiceNow reported a subscription revenue growth of 21.0%, which was once again above the company's previously issued guidance of 19.5%. What is even more impressive is achieving this kind of growth at scale, as NOW's total revenues amounted to $13.3 billion for the full year of 2025. If the rise of artificial intelligence were to really pose an existential risk to the company's business model, I would expect the revenue growth rates to disappoint on the downside, not the opposite.

Intuit is also operating at scale, with total revenues of $21.1 billion expected in fiscal 2026. This would represent another year of top-line growth in the mid-teens with a non-GAAP operating margin of around 40%. As discussed in my previous article about Adobe, non-GAAP earnings exclude the impact of share-based compensation, which is a significant expense for most software companies, including Intuit. Inclusive of a share-based compensation expense of $2.0 billion, Intuit's operating margin drops to 26%, which is still impressive and more representative of the underlying economic reality. Share-based compensation might be of a non-cash nature, but it is still an expense that must be accounted for.

On one hand, Adobe is aggressively buying back shares, but on the other hand, the effect of those buybacks is in part offset by the issuance of shares to employees and executives. In fiscal 2025 alone, the company's share-based compensation amounted to $1.9 billion, leaving $9.4 billion worth of share repurchases on a net basis.

In Intuit's case, the best way to highlight the real cost of share-based compensation is to look at the amount of share buyback relative to the number of diluted shares outstanding. For example, Intuit deployed $2.0 billion toward share buybacks in both fiscal 2023 and fiscal 2024 on top of another $2.8 billion in fiscal 2025, for a three-year total of $6.7 billion. Over the same period, the number of diluted shares outstanding remains almost exactly the same, from 284 million at the end of 2022 to 283 million at the end of 2025. How can we explain the fact that after $6.7 billion worth of share buybacks, the number of diluted shares outstanding remains pretty much the same? This is when the long-term cost of share-based compensation becomes real.

From this perspective, the free cash flow figures illustrated in the chart above are somewhat misleading, as Intuit's share repurchases should be viewed as an expense to keep the number of shares outstanding stable instead of a return of excess capital to shareholders.

Valuation

After adjusting for a share-based compensation expense of $2.0 billion in fiscal 2025, Intuit's free cash flows amount to approximately $4.1 billion, which is still substantial. Relative to INTU's current market capitalization of $111.1 billion, it represents a yield of ~3.7%, which is pretty good once the top-line growth in the mid-teens is taken into account. Looking at S&P's (SPGI) recent credit upgrade of Intuit, we can see that the rating agencies expect free cash flows to reach $7.5 billion in fiscal 2027. Assuming a constant share-based compensation expense, this would translate into free cash flows of approximately $5.5 billion, for a forward free cash flow yield of ~5.0%.

Even though the chart below does not take into account the impact of share-based compensation, it still shows that Intuit is trading at the highest free cash flow yield in over a decade. Even if the free cash flow yield drops to about 3.7% once dilution is taken into account, the general pattern remains the same, as it would also be the highest yield in over a decade.

Risk Factors

Even if I expect Intuit to benefit from the structural trend toward AI agents, nobody can know for sure the ultimate impact it will have on the company's business model over the long term. For now, given the recent sell-off, market participants are voting with their dollars that it will be a net negative, and they could be right. At the end of the day, this is why financial markets exist, and if the ultimate impact ends up being positive, as I believe, Intuit's shares are a bargain right now.

With a declining share price, the dilutive impact of a ~$2.0 billion share-based compensation expense increases. For Intuit's shareholders, this risk has largely been offset in the past by the company's recurrent share buybacks financed by internally generated cash flows. As discussed in this article, I believe it is an important element to take into account when looking at the financial performance of any software company, as this is not a risk specific to Intuit.

Q2-26 Earnings

Intuit is set to report its earnings for the second quarter of fiscal 2026 on February 26. Based on the company's guidance released last quarter, revenue growth in Q2-26 is expected to be between 14% and 15%, with GAAP and non-GAAP earnings per share of $1.79 and $3.66 at the midpoint, respectively.

In my view, the only thing Intuit has to do for the market to react positively is simply to meet its previously announced guidance for Q2-26 and reiterate the full-year guidance for fiscal 2026. Considering how severe the sell-off has been on the stock, and how expectations have been revised lower, the bar is quite low at the moment. From this perspective, Q2-26 earnings could act as a catalyst to create a bottom on the share price. If Intuit meets its guidance, it could reassure the street and provide additional support to the view that AI-related fears are grossly overestimated when it comes to Intuit's software suite.

Conclusion

In my opinion, the ongoing 'SaaS-pocalypse' creates the perfect opportunity to acquire shares of a high-quality compounder at a discount. After reaching an all-time high of $813.70 in July 2025, INTU's share price reached a multi-year low of $389.32 last week, a decline of more than 50% in just a few months. As discussed in this article, not only do I view the ongoing AI-related fears as overstated, but I actually believe that Intuit is well-positioned to benefit from the structural trend toward AI agents.

With an unparalleled amount of high-quality data to train large language models and build AI agents, Intuit sits at the forefront of the AI revolution. It is precisely by using Intuit's products that small and medium-sized businesses can harness the production gains associated with AI. The commoditization of coding may have lowered the barriers to entry of the software business, but there are still many hurdles to cross before the foundations of enterprises like Intuit start to shake.

From a strategic point of view, I consider that Intuit and ServiceNow are particularly well-positioned in regard to AI, while disruption risks may be more elevated for a company like Adobe, hence its much higher free cash flow yield. Overall, I believe it is a great time to invest in the software industry while keeping in mind that certain business models will indeed be disrupted. There will be winners and losers, and making the difference between the two is key in this environment, as the selling has been pretty much indiscriminate so far.

It is my personal belief that Intuit will emerge as a winner in a world increasingly influenced by artificial intelligence; hence, my 'Strong Buy' rating. In every challenge lies the seed of a new opportunity, and I believe this applies to what Intuit is facing at the moment. The company's financial performance remains exceptional, with top-line growth in the low teens and significant free cash flow generation. The most negative aspect around Intuit seems to be about market sentiment, but fears are what they are; they also contain the seed of greed. Be greedy when others are fearful, remember the old saying?

data.title: Alger International Opportunities Fund Q4 2025: Winners And Laggards
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Alger International Opportunities Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

Class A shares of the Alger International Opportunities Fund underperformed the MSCI ACWI ex USA Index during the fourth quarter of 2025. The Alger International Opportunities Fund primarily seeks to derive performance from stock specific sources. Returns from individual stock selection detracted from relative performance, while factor exposures had a positive impact. The portfolio's overweighting of style factors such as medium-term momentum and exchange rate sensitivity were the most significant contributors to performance. On the other hand, the portfolio's underweight to dividend yield and overweight to value were the most significant detractors from performance during the quarter.

Among countries, South Korea, Italy, and Spain provided the largest contributions to relative performance, while Japan, the United Kingdom, and the Netherlands were the most notable detractors from relative performance.

Contributors to Performance

Heidelberg Materials AG (HLBZY) ('HLBZY'), Hitachi, Ltd. (HTHIY), and SK hynix Inc. (HXSCL) ('HXSCL') were among the top relative contributors to performance.

  • Heidelberg Materials is a global building materials company that produces and supplies aggregates, cement, and ready-mixed concrete for construction projects worldwide, with a strong international presence and a focus on sustainable building solutions. Shares contributed positively during the quarter, driven by strong pricing discipline, margin expansion, and visible self-help cost savings that more than offset softer volumes in parts of Europe. A favorable regional mix—particularly higher-margin contributions from Africa, the Mediterranean, and Group Services—supported earnings resilience, while stable energy costs reduced volatility versus peers. In parallel, early monetization of its decarbonization leadership and premium products strengthened investor confidence into year-end.
  • Hitachi is a global industrial and technology company with leading positions in digital systems, energy, rail, and industrial automation, with an increasing emphasis on software-enabled solutions through its Lumada platform. We believe the investment case is supported by Hitachi's ongoing portfolio shift toward higher-margin digital and infrastructure businesses, improving returns on invested capital, and long-duration exposure to secular themes such as electrification, automation, and data-driven infrastructure spending. Shares contributed positively during the quarter after the company reported better-than-expected earnings, supported by strong demand across its digital solutions and energy infrastructure businesses, which led management to raise full-year fiscal 2025 guidance.
  • SK hynix is a leading memory manufacturer, producing DRAM and NAND flash used in smartphones, servers, and PCs. We believe the company is a leading supplier of high-bandwidth memory ('HBM'), a critical component in AI servers and accelerator systems. By focusing R&D on advanced HBM, SK hynix has gained share, improved average selling prices, and delivered stronger-than-expected revenue and margin growth as AI demand has accelerated. Shares contributed positively during the quarter as the company reported strong earnings growth that was significantly higher than consensus estimates, driven primarily by robust demand for its HBM products.
  • Xiaomi (XIACY) is one of China's leading consumer technology companies, selling smartphones and a broad suite of connected lifestyle products, while also expanding into internet services and smart electric vehicles (EVs). The company's "Human x Car x Home" strategy is designed to knit these offerings into a single ecosystem, using AI and smart connectivity to deepen user engagement and drive growth across its segments. data.title: The Mag 7 Hit A Critical Level
data.description:
With a sell-off in big tech stocks coming along with the current market rotation, we've seen the ratio of the Magnificent Seven stocks to the broader market slip. The average stock in the S&P 500 has outperformed the S&P 500 YTD, which is a rare occurrence in modern markets, which have largely been driven by these massive firms.

SA Quant Sours on the Mag 7

This was the first warning sign for me, as SA Quant has turned neutral on the entirety of the Mag 7.

Analysts are still largely bullish on these firms—bar Apple (AAPL) and Tesla (TSLA)—but SA Quant has downgraded the lot to a hold for now.

While it is unclear to me why SA Quant gives the ending grade that it does, since its weights are hidden to the public, we can see the ratings for each component.

What is clear is that Quant's largest problem with these stocks is their valuations. Most of them fail on this metric, with Microsoft (MSFT) scoring a D- and Amazon (AMZN) scoring a D+. That's still failing if we're going by university standards here. Not a good look.

These tech firms have been heavy for the indexes, as they contribute so much of the weight, accounting for up to 35% of the index among just those 7 stocks, while the rest is split among the other 493 stocks. This is complicated by their high valuations, which disproportionately weigh on the S&P 500.

The difference is stark to see when you put it like this: most of the stocks in the S&P 500 have lower forward price-to-earnings ratios than the index has by weight. Since these fast-growing tech giants own so much of the index by weight and have incredibly high expectations placed on them by the market—translating into high P/E ratios—they pull the index valuations up.

A re-rating here may be a good thing, especially if the bull market is to continue. So far this year, tech, communications, and discretionary firms (basically the sectors that encompass the whole Mag 7) have all seen valuation compression. Their prices are down, but their earnings per share are up!

The Mag 7 Hits Major Technical Support

That sell-off is now hitting a critical level that investors should be watching, not a fundamental level, but a technical one. The technical picture for the Mag 7 is easy to track—there's an ETF for that, the Roundhill Magnificent Seven ETF (MAGS).

It just broke through its 200-day moving average and is approaching the anchored VWAP from its last deep lows in April, which should now create a strong support level.

Having broken through the 200-day moving average is damning evidence enough that investors are seriously worried about the Mag 7 and their prospects, trading down below that level of support. This next level is make or break for the firms; they need to hold the ~$60 level, or we should really watch out below.

If the fund falls below that mark, we can assume that there is no fundamental source of demand, and we could see a return to lows another 10% deeper, marking a bear market for this basket of stocks. Consider the stakes here to be fairly high, at least from a technical perspective.

So far, we've seen the fund respect that line, bouncing off it following the sell-off from earlier in the week. The lows seen on February 17th hit just above the $60 mark but recovered shortly after.

This marks what I believe to be a bottom for the Mag 7, at least in the short term (weeks). I expect the market to continue to respect that $60 level moving forward, and another rally could pull support up even further. That is something to watch for.

On the other hand, if I'm wrong here and the narrative around the Magnificent 7 being overvalued and needing a lot more correction in this ongoing market rotation is true, we stand to fall into bear territory from the last high.

If that happens, I would expect the rotation trade to light up even more. Expect outperformance of the S&P 500 by the Dow and by the Russell 2000.

The Market Still Cares About One Thing

I'm on the record for saying that my bullishness on the Mag 7 as a general basket and low marginal cost businesses in general (tech, comm services, etc.) is largely hinged on the thing that makes them special in the first place: profit margin expansion.

So long as this remains intact, I can see why now would be a local bottom for these firms. Here's a chart I keep coming back to on this point—there's been a clear correlation between forward profit margins and average valuations for the last two decades.

On this point, no matter how much pain the stocks have undergone this year, the Mag 7 really impress (except Tesla). Gross profit margins and net income margins are incredibly high; there's a good reason SA Quant gives all seven of them an A+ in this category.

Conclusion

The Magnificent 7 have seen their stock prices fall and valuations re-rated this year as part of the broader rotation that is currently ongoing in markets. The fall has now taken them to a crucial point, bouncing off the trend established in the last crash in April 2025.

For the MAGS ETF that tracks the Mag 7 stocks, this level sits at the $60 mark and is a pivot point for short-term trading. If the fund closes below that level, I would expect more bearishness to follow and for investors to need to “look out below” for these prolific stocks.

The long-term fundamental trends are still seemingly intact, with profitability (what I see as one of the key drivers of valuations in today's market) scoring excellent marks across the board with their margins still growing. This creates opportunity, as I see it.

I expect this to be a relative bottom for the Mag 7, which begets bullishness for the broad market, as they drive the larger trend in the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq 100.

Thanks for reading.

Market Environment

During the fourth quarter of 2025, the MSCI ACWI ex USA rose 5.11% in U.S. dollar terms, with the Materials and Information Technology sectors outperforming the index, while the Consumer Discretionary and Communication Services sectors underperformed. Style factors such as size and value posted outsized returns, while liquidity and volatility factors lagged. Non-U.S. equity markets advanced, supported by the Federal Reserve's (Fed) well-telegraphed rate cuts in October and December, along with growing enthusiasm for artificial intelligence ('AI'), which boosted broader risk appetite.

European equities posted strong gains during the quarter, supported by outperformance in Financials as easing financial conditions and lower interest rates improved the outlook for lending and credit quality across the region. Resilient labor markets and moderating inflation further reinforced the Eurozone's macro backdrop, supporting investor confidence.

Emerging market equities rose in the quarter, supported by strong technology-led gains in South Korea and Taiwan and an easier U.S. policy backdrop as the Fed cut interest rates. Equities in South Korea advanced on surging demand for AI-related memory hardware, with additional support from Industrials amid a new U.S. trade deal that included tariff reductions and significant direct investment. In Taiwan, equities benefited from continued demand for AI-related semiconductors. Equities in China fell in U.S. dollar terms as concerns grew over softer economic data, ongoing property-market stress, and subdued investor sentiment.

In Japan, equities advanced as optimism around AI and higher defense spending supported investor sentiment. Additionally, Sanae Takaichi's election as prime minister and the formation of a coalition between the Liberal Democratic Party and the Japan Innovation Party were seen as signs of greater political stability and a higher likelihood of proactive fiscal support.

Class A shares of the Alger International Opportunities Fund underperformed the MSCI ACWI ex USA Index during the fourth quarter of 2025.
